findings.summary: Stage method docblock says 'placeholders' but methods are fully implemented
evidence: Line 370-371: '# Stage execution methods - these will call the actual scripts/commands\n# For now, these are placeholders that will be implemented to call'  — all methods are fully implemented; this is stale scaffolding commentary that misrepresents the code state
findings.suggestion: Remove the stale placeholder comment block above the stage methods

findings.summary: infrastructure/reporting/ contains 14 files with overlapping report-generation responsibilities
evidence: pipeline_reporter.py defines generate_pipeline_report, generate_validation_report, generate_performance_report, save_error_summary — four distinct report domains in one file
suite_summary_generator.py defines generate_summary_report and generate_markdown_report covering a fifth domain
coverage_reporter.py and coverage_parser.py split coverage concerns across two files with no clear ownership boundary
findings.suggestion: Group by output domain: one module per report type (pipeline, tests, validation, coverage). The current fan-out makes it hard to locate which module owns which report.

findings.summary: LLMConfig.from_env() is called 5+ times inside a single function body and across tightly coupled call sites in generator.py
evidence: generate_review_with_metrics() calls LLMConfig.from_env() at line 571 (for max_tokens default) and again at line 599 (inside the retry loop for every attempt) — two env-parse calls per attempt
extract_manuscript_text() calls LLMConfig.from_env() at line 470 independently
generate_translation() calls LLMConfig.from_env() at line 726 as _cfg, duplicating what create_review_client already constructed
Total of 5 separate LLMConfig.from_env() calls within the same module's execution path for a single review run
findings.suggestion: Construct LLMConfig once at the call site that owns the review session (e.g. the orchestrator that calls generate_review_with_metrics) and pass it in, rather than re-parsing the environment repeatedly inside the function body and its retry loop.

findings.summary: generate_review_with_metrics() handles prompt building, retry logic, streaming, validation, and deduplication in one body
evidence: Lines 557-653: single function does: template instantiation, prompt rendering, model-size detection, retry loop, streaming via _stream_with_heartbeat, quality validation, off-topic flag tracking, best-response tracking, post-generation deduplication, and metrics assembly
The retry loop at lines 590-628 contains its own prompt mutation (_build_retry_prompt) and temperature escalation inline
findings.suggestion: Extract retry orchestration into a dedicated _execute_with_retry helper; keep generate_review_with_metrics responsible only for prompt assembly and metrics. Post-generation deduplication can be a post-processing step called by the orchestrator.

findings.summary: report_results() in scripts/01_run_tests.py duplicates the infra_was_run detection logic that was already computed above it
evidence: Line 348: infra_was_run = infra_results.get('total', 0) > 0 or infra_exit != 0 computed for the first time

Line 422: infra_was_run = infra_total > 0 or infra_exit != 0 — identical expression recomputed in the same function, shadowing the first binding with a new variable name (infra_total vs the earlier direct call)
findings.suggestion: Compute infra_was_run once at the top of report_results() using infra_results and infra_exit, then reference that single binding throughout.

findings.summary: optimizer.py has disproportionately large docstrings relative to function complexity
evidence: gradient_descent() has a 50-line docstring (lines 189-245) for a ~60-line implementation; the docstring includes a full 'Infrastructure Integration' section with multi-paragraph usage examples that restate what is already documented in the module-level docstring (lines 1-37)
quadratic_function() and compute_gradient() each have 25+ line docstrings for 10-line implementations
The module-level docstring (37 lines) repeats the infrastructure integration example that also appears verbatim in gradient_descent()'s docstring
findings.suggestion: Consolidate infrastructure integration examples to the module docstring only; trim function docstrings to the Args/Returns/Raises contract. The usage examples inside function docstrings are redundant with the module-level section.

findings.summary: _parse_failures_section() uses fragile string heuristics that overlap with downstream fallback strategies
evidence: Lines 43-63: the section parser detects test names by matching lines that start with '__' and contain '__', then separately looks for lines starting with '/' and '.py:' — this pattern is specific to --tb=line output but is listed first in _FAILURE_STRATEGIES, meaning it runs even for verbose/short output and returns an empty list rather than None, causing the cascade to short-circuit at the first non-empty result
extract_failed_tests() returns on the first non-empty result (line 138-139), but _parse_failures_section can return partial results for verbose output that don't capture the test name correctly
findings.suggestion: Either add a format-detection gate before the cascade (detect which pytest output format is present) or change the cascade to return only when a result passes a minimum-confidence threshold rather than stopping at the first non-empty list.

findings.summary: generate_executive_summary, generate_quality_review, generate_methodology_review, generate_improvement_suggestions are pass-through wrappers with no added logic
evidence: Lines 656-713: all four functions have identical structure — they call generate_review_with_metrics with hardcoded review_type, review_name, template_class, temperature, and max_tokens=None
No conditional logic, no pre/post processing, no policy enforcement; they are purely named aliases for generate_review_with_metrics calls

generate_translation() at line 716 is structurally different — it does not route through generate_review_with_metrics and duplicates the streaming, metrics, and error-handling blocks inline — breaking the pattern established by the four review wrappers
findings.suggestion: Either (a) make generate_translation consistent with the wrapper pattern by routing through generate_review_with_metrics, or (b) collapse the four review wrappers into a data-driven dispatch table keyed on review type. The current split between pass-through wrappers and an inline reimplementation is inconsistent.

findings.summary: run_infrastructure_tests and run_project_tests handle the quiet flag inconsistently
evidence: run_infrastructure_tests() passes quiet to TestSuiteConfig but also unconditionally appends '-q' to the pytest cmd at line 136 (cmd.extend(['-q'])), regardless of the quiet parameter value
run_project_tests() appends '-q' only when quiet is True (line 239: if quiet: cmd.extend(['-q'])), which is the correct conditional pattern
The two sibling functions implement the same quiet-flag responsibility with different control logic
findings.suggestion: Apply the same conditional pattern in run_infrastructure_tests: append '-q' only when quiet is True, consistent with run_project_tests.

findings.summary: pipeline_reporter.py mixes open()/write() and Path.write_text() for file output without a consistent pattern
evidence: save_pipeline_report() uses open(json_path, 'w') with json.dump for JSON (line 117) but uses md_path.write_text(md_content) for Markdown (line 129) and html_path.write_text for HTML (line 140)
generate_validation_report() uses open(json_path, 'w') + json.dump for JSON (line 444) and md_path.write_text for Markdown (line 455) — same mixed pattern
generate_performance_report() uses open(json_path, 'w') + json.dump (line 491)
save_error_summary() uses open(json_path, 'w') + json.dump and md_path.write_text — consistent within itself but the json/markdown write-method divergence is present across all four functions
findings.suggestion: Standardize on Path.write_text() throughout for text output, using json.dumps(data, indent=2) to produce the string first. This removes the inconsistency between the json and non-json paths.
